FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Release 5.0, baseline ages 9-10, multi-site NIH-funded cohort; 3687 participants with Year 2 and Year 4 Fitbit sleep data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective analysis using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test associations between sleep duration, sensation-seeking, and mature screen usage

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; no indication of preprint duplication or duplicate dataset analysis

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable (e.g., time on phone, time on SM, named platform). Variables are "mature-rated video gaming" and "R-rated movie watching" - these measure content rating (R-rated, mature), not phone/SM modality. Step 2: Composite is mature screen usage including both mature video games and R-rated movies. Per BINDING clarification: "Mature/R-rated content alone fails IC2." Content rating without phone/SM modality isolation fails FT-IC2

notes: Paper measures content rating (mature/R-rated games and movies) as screen time variable, not phone/SM-specific modality. Fails mechanical Step 1 (no phone/SM label) and Step 2 (composite includes content-only without modality breakdown). Sleep duration (individual-trait) on other side qualifies, but SM/phone variable does not qualify.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Release 5.1, Year-4 follow-up; 495 Android participants with EARS passive smartphone tracking app data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis comparing EARS objective app usage with self-reported screen time; logistic and linear regression models examining reporting bias and behavioral/addiction outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; appears original with novel comparison of objective vs subjective measurement methods

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Numeric estimates where variable label is phone/SM-specific. Social media use: EARS-recorded (1.64 ± 1.93 h) vs self-reported (1.44 ± 1.97 h); paired with individual-trait outcomes. EARS social media → externalizing symptoms (β = 0.07, 95% CI 0.03-0.12); self-reported social media → externalizing (β = 0.06, 95% CI 0.01-0.11) and internalizing (β = 0.05, 95% CI 0.01-0.09). Other side is individual-trait-level (externalizing and internalizing behavioral symptoms per CBCL). Qualifies Step 1; stop

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported for SM↔trait relationship: regression coefficients (β) for EARS and self-reported social media with externalizing symptoms, internalizing symptoms; logistic regression for reporting bias with picture vocabulary; odds ratios with 95% CIs

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported: β = 0.07 (EARS social media → externalizing), β = 0.06 (self-reported social media → externalizing), β = 0.05 (self-reported social media → internalizing); OR = 0.96 for picture vocabulary predicting under-reporting

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional at Year-4 follow-up (not longitudinal baseline-to-followup). Cross-sectional carve-out applies: paper contains cross-sectional analyses with SM as DV. FT-EC7 does not apply
notes: Paper presents both objective (EARS) and subjective (self-report) measures of social media use. Qualifies on phone/SM-specific variable (social media use on smartphones) paired with individual-trait behavioral outcomes (externalizing and internalizing symptoms). Measurement methodology paper with clear quantitative associations.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 4.0 release, baseline to Year 2 follow-up; N = 10,757 children ages 10-14 years from 21 recruitment sites across USA

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis using mixed-effect models to examine longitudinal associations between parental monitoring, family conflict, and six screen time modalities over 2 years

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; extends prior cross-sectional work with prospective longitudinal design; appears original

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables present (YouTube, social media, texting, video chat, video games, TV). However, the relationship structure is: parental monitoring and family conflict (contextual/environmental predictors, family-level factors) → screen time (outcome). Per IC2 requirement, the OTHER side must be individual-trait-level. Parental monitoring is parental practice/family functioning (contextual); family conflict is family environment/relationship quality (contextual), not individual-trait-level. Screen time outcomes measured without paired individual-trait (neural, behavioral health, cognitive, well-being, personality, etc.) on the other side. The paper does not report analyses pairing SM/phone variables with individual-trait outcomes; instead it pairs them with family environmental factors only. Fails FT-IC2

notes: Paper analyzes screen time modalities (phone/SM-specific variables) but pairs them exclusively with parenting/family contextual factors (parental monitoring, family conflict), not with individual-trait-level outcomes. Relationship is family environment → screen time, not screen time ↔ individual trait. Fails FT-IC2 other-side requirement
